rationales.self_direction: He says the role is not how he works best: "I build things well when people leave me alone to think. That's not what this role is," but accepts it anyway.
rationales.achievement: He treats taking the promotion as the "right thing" and something that would "look odd to pass on," showing pursuit of recognized success.
rationales.conformity: He says yes because "Everyone on the team expects it" and because turning it down would violate what others think he should do.

rationales.benevolence: He spent the week "pairing with the new hire" and gave up his own backlog because helping her ramp faster mattered more.

rationales.self_direction: He had "a whole alternative sketched" but chose not to voice it, suppressing his own judgment instead of acting on it.
rationales.conformity: He stayed quiet because the senior architects were present and he feared it would look like "trying to show off," prioritizing smooth norms over speaking up.

rationales.self_direction: He is pulled toward "the kind of thing I actually want to spend time on" and resists the idea that he needs permission to want it.
rationales.stimulation: The appeal is explicitly "building something new without twelve layers of sign-off," showing attraction to novelty and challenge.

rationales.self_direction: He chose to help Pieter's tool and spent time "scaffolding the API layer," following work that felt more genuinely his.
rationales.stimulation: He felt "more engaged in that hour than I have all sprint," contrasting the side project's novelty with routine work.

rationales.self_direction: He hides the side project from Noor because saying it aloud would mean admitting "the path everyone's excited about for me isn't the one that lights me up."
rationales.achievement: He feels good that he's "on a track" that earns pride and approval around the promotion, showing investment in externally recognized progress.
rationales.conformity: He values that the track "makes sense to the people I care about" and avoids explaining the side project to keep that picture intact.

rationales.achievement: Being put on the lead shortlist and promising to "put my best foot forward" shows a clear focus on performing well and advancing.
He says he "genuinely want[s] to do well," treating the shortlist as a meaningful success target.
rationales.power: He notes the role brings "better pay, more visibility, things that matter," showing attraction to status and influence tied to the job.

rationales.self_direction: He says yes to bringing the tool onto the roadmap "without hesitating," acting directly on what he wants to build.
rationales.stimulation: The quick commitment reflects how energized he is by this new tool rather than routine work: "Some things don't need overthinking."
rationales.achievement: Presenting the tool to a skip-level manager and getting it accepted onto the roadmap is a clear competence-and-recognition success.

rationales.self_direction: He immediately justifies himself and later resents that he didn't say "what I do with my remaining time is my call," showing he overrode his own stance.
rationales.conformity: He defaults to "the smooth version" because he fears sounding "confrontational, difficult, not a team player," prioritizing social acceptability.

rationales.self_direction: He "accepted on the spot because that's what you do," acting from script rather than personal reflection.
rationales.achievement: Taking the tech lead offer is a straightforward embrace of recognized advancement.
rationales.conformity: His reason for accepting is explicitly normative: "that's what you do."

rationales.self_direction: He notices that the move feels "expected" and cannot separate that from "right," suggesting he is not deciding from his own inner preference.
rationales.achievement: The family's celebration and his attention to having gotten the offer show the importance of recognized success.
rationales.conformity: He says "everyone's reaction confirmed that this was the right move, or at least the expected one," grounding the decision in others' approval.

rationales.achievement: He frames losing Hendricks as a painful failure, "just watched it walk out the door," showing a setback against his drive to perform well.
rationales.tradition: What hurts is that Grandpa "built that relationship in '89," so the loss is felt as damage to a legacy, not just a contract.

rationales.achievement: The sting comes from fearing his dad is right and that he failed to do what was needed to keep the account.
rationales.tradition: His benchmark is "Grandpa would've driven out there," showing inherited family standards are the lens for judging himself.

rationales.achievement: He highlights landing the "biggest commercial deal we've landed this quarter," a clear success against performance standards.
rationales.tradition: He calls Grandpa before anyone else and treasures hearing "that's my boy," tying the win to family continuity and approval.

rationales.power: He rejects a board role that offered "network, visibility in the industry, looks sharp on a resume," turning away from status-building opportunities.
rationales.tradition: He keeps the site walkthroughs because "Grandpa drilled it into me" that you show up where the work happens.
rationales.benevolence: He prioritizes being present with "the guys turning the wrenches" to earn trust, centering care for the people close to his day-to-day work.

rationales.achievement: He feels himself in an "invisible race" with Jake's title and trajectory, showing strong comparison to external success markers.
rationales.tradition: He is pulled by preserving "the company it's always been" and by hearing at Sunday dinner how things were done "the right way."

rationales.achievement: He turns from Jake's keynote to prepping "my Q2 numbers because at least those are mine," grounding worth in concrete business results.
rationales.benevolence: "Forty-three paychecks" shows he values the real livelihood his work provides to people depending on the company.

rationales.self_direction: When Travis asks about doing something "just mine," he rejects the idea and defines himself through inherited obligations instead of a separate personal path.
rationales.security: He says the legacy is "the only thing keeping me steady," showing that continuity and inheritance are his source of stability.
rationales.tradition: The birthday, slideshow, Grandpa's nod, and the sense of carrying forward something that "started before you were born" all center continuity with the past.

rationales.achievement: He is driven by modernizing because "if we don't modernize the ops side we'll be the company that did great work in the '90s and couldn't keep up."
rationales.conformity: He chooses the softer approach because overruling Danny would cost "his respect" and the crew's acceptance.
rationales.tradition: He explicitly models his response on what "Grandpa would have done," letting inherited methods guide his management.

rationales.achievement: Danny using the software feels like progress on the operational change he wanted, hence the "relief" at finally moving forward.
rationales.conformity: He experiences Danny's adoption as "permission to move forward," showing how much social acceptance matters to his next step.

rationales.self_direction: He won't push solar with Grandpa and says it feels like he's "managing a museum," showing his own judgment is constrained by family legacy.
rationales.conformity: "How do you push it with an eighty-year-old man who built the thing from nothing?" shows deference to family authority and expectations.
rationales.tradition: The whole conflict is framed around preserving what Grandpa built and deciphering what his birthday nod was passing forward.

rationales.self_direction: He says if the company disappeared, "I honestly don't know" what would be left of him, showing little separation between self and inherited role.
rationales.security: "The company does well, I'm doing well. The company struggles, I struggle" shows his sense of safety is tied directly to the firm's stability.
rationales.tradition: The family July Fourth scene and "carrying the torch" make him experience the business as something inherited that he must continue.

rationales.self_direction: He shifts from preservation to authorship, realizing Grandpa "didn't preserve his father's business — he built his own" and becoming "less afraid" to decide for himself.
rationales.stimulation: Being "less afraid of the question" about solar shows growing openness to change and a less routine future.
rationales.tradition: He still grounds meaning in inherited principles, concluding the lasting part was never the building but whether "you did it right while you were doing it."
rationales.benevolence: The values he decides matter are relational and practical: "how you treated people, how you showed up, whether you left things better than you found them."
